summary: Cathy O'Neil enthusiastically reviews the Head First Java book, praising its unconventional teaching approach with quirky cartoons and engaging style. She notes she's reading it to understand object-oriented programming and bridge to courses her students will take. While impressed by the book's ability to make technical content a 'thrilling read,' she criticizes its sexist references to women in tight sweaters as programmer motivation. She also mentions Head First Design Patterns and declares she'll never write technical books in the 'old-fashioned dry way' again.
key_points: Head First books use quirky cartoons and humor to make technical content engaging and memorable
The book explicitly uses emotional attachment to help readers remember technical details
The series contains sexist elements, using 'women in tight sweaters' as assumed programmer motivation
Programming is just a toolset - learning to think about constructing with those tools (design patterns) is the real goal
Traditional dry technical writing may be inferior to more engaging approaches
money_quotes: I'm on page 213 after less than a week, and yes that's out of more than 600 pages but what I'm saying is that it's a thrilling read.
My one complaint is how often the book talks about motivating programmers with women in tight sweaters.

At the beginning they made the point that people remember stuff better when there is emotional attachment to things, so I'm guessing they're getting me annoyed to help me remember details on reference types.
Ultimately, programming is just a tool set and you need to learn how to think about constructing stuff with those tools.
Honestly I will never write a technical book in the old-fashioned dry way again.
key_insight: Technical education can be dramatically more effective when it's engaging and emotionally resonant, though the tech industry's assumptions about who programmers are often leak into even progressive educational materials.
